video_title: Gene Munster Believes It's Different This Time
gist: Gene Munster (Deep Water) argues the AI buildout is genuinely 'different this time' and we're only in the second inning of wealth creation, using Micron's surge to 16 five-year strategic customer agreements as evidence demand is structurally non-cyclical, while flagging Apple's just-announced ~15% price hikes (driven by Micron's memory costs) as a misunderstood positive. He predicts 40-60% hyperscaler capex growth in 2027 (well above ~30% consensus), claims we are effectively at AGI, sees Google as the best AI capital allocator, and bets Tesla folds under SpaceX within three years.

insights.claim: Micron's jump from one to 16 strategic customer agreements (SCAs), including roughly six new five-year deals with large customers, signals customers see no end to their memory appetite and pushes the next supply-demand equilibrium out to 2028 or 2029.
insights.speaker: Gene Munster
insights.so_what: Long-dated customer lock-ins are the bull case that memory's boom-bust cycle is structurally extended; watch SCA count as the key tell for the AI memory trade.
insights.quote: the number of SCAS went from one announce last quarter to 16 in total. So there's 15 incremental.

insights.claim: Customers would never sign five-year memory deals if they thought the cycle were near an end, so the SCAs are a direct signal from the buyers that AI memory demand has years left to run.
insights.speaker: Gene Munster
insights.so_what: Frames customer behavior, not Micron's guidance, as the cleanest demand signal for the broader AI hardware trade.
insights.quote: If they thought we were near an end, that's the last thing you want to do is send a five-year deal.

insights.claim: Micron's stock is up roughly 1,000-1,200% over the past year while its multiple has stayed unchanged, meaning the entire move has been driven by ~1,000% earnings growth rather than multiple expansion.
insights.speaker: Gene Munster
insights.so_what: Implies the stock is not expensive on the move; multiple re-rating remains a latent upside catalyst if investor scar tissue fades.
insights.quote: the multiple has been unchanged over the past year. So that means that earnings have gone up basically a thousand%.

insights.claim: Investor 'muscle memory' and scar tissue around memory's historical boom-bust mean Micron will likely never earn a respectable multiple even though its earnings power keeps driving the stock higher.
insights.speaker: Gene Munster
insights.so_what: Suggests playing Micron on earnings momentum, not re-rating, and tempers upside targets despite strong fundamentals.
insights.quote: I don't think Micron's going to get a like a respectable multiple probably ever. But I do think that the earnings power uh will continue to uh drive this higher

insights.claim: Memory supply is effectively a duopoly between Micron and SK Hynix because it takes many years for other manufacturers to ramp, giving incumbents durable pricing power.
insights.speaker: Gene Munster
insights.so_what: High barriers to entry support sustained memory pricing power that is being captured at customers' margin expense.
insights.quote: It's really them and SKH High that are kind of running the show here and it takes so many years to get up to speed for other manufacturers.

insights.claim: Micron's margins went from negative in 2023 with the company losing money to roughly 80%+ gross margins and an expected ~$100 of earnings, with sales projected to 10x from 2023 levels.
insights.speaker: Dan Nathan
insights.so_what: The swing quantifies how much AI memory pricing power is being extracted from downstream customers like device and server makers.
insights.quote: They had negative margins in 2023. The company was losing money and now they're expected to have $100 in earnings.

insights.claim: Roughly 50% of Micron's revenue comes from the hyperscalers, making hyperscaler capex the most direct proxy for memory demand.
insights.speaker: Gene Munster
insights.so_what: Ties Micron's fortunes tightly to hyperscaler capex trajectory, the key variable to monitor for the trade.
insights.quote: That's 50% of Micron's revenue is the hyperscalers.

insights.claim: Munster expects 2027 hyperscaler capex to grow 40-60%, well above an optimistic consensus near 30% and above the Street's ~22% baseline, with Nvidia guided to ~40% growth per Jensen's comments.
insights.speaker: Gene Munster
insights.so_what: An explicitly out-of-consensus capex call; if right, it is bullish for the entire AI hardware supply chain into 2027.
insights.quote: I think that the capex for next year is probably going to grow somewhere between 40 and 60%. I mean meaningfully higher

insights.claim: Calendar 2026 hyperscaler capex grew roughly 80%, so even the ~22% Street estimate for 2027 represents a meaningful step down that Munster believes is too pessimistic.
insights.speaker: Gene Munster
insights.so_what: The gap between an 80%-to-22% deceleration narrative and Munster's 40-60% call defines the key debate for AI hardware stocks.
insights.quote: the shoe is looking for 22% growth in hyperscalers capex. Now that compares to about 80% growth in calendar 26.

insights.claim: Energy availability, not chips or capital, is the biggest rate governor on the AI buildout going forward.
insights.speaker: Gene Munster
insights.so_what: Points investors toward power generation and energy infrastructure as the binding constraint and potential bottleneck-driven trade.
insights.quote: I think the biggest rate uh governor on this whole thing is going to be around energy.

insights.claim: The AI hardware stack (Micron, Nvidia, data centers, energy) is the 'brain' that keeps getting bigger, while inference and the value-creation layer have barely started beyond experimentation.
insights.speaker: Gene Munster
insights.so_what: Implies the next leg of value migrates from infrastructure to the inference/application layer that is still untapped.
insights.quote: the inference part really hasn't started... but the brain just keeps getting bigger and bigger.

insights.claim: Munster believes this AI cycle is genuinely 'different this time,' explicitly invoking the most dangerous phrase in investing to argue AI's substance will exceed even the hype.
insights.speaker: Gene Munster
insights.so_what: A high-conviction, against-his-own-caution stance; sizing the AI trade hinges on whether you share this belief.
insights.quote: I think it's going to be different this time.

insights.claim: Munster argues we are in only the second inning not just of AI technology but of wealth creation, predicting many AI-linked stocks can still rise 10x over the next five years even after 1,000% runs.
insights.speaker: Gene Munster
insights.so_what: Reframes already-extended winners as still early; the boldest claim in the episode and the core risk/reward for staying long.
insights.quote: the fact that there's still room for many stocks to go up by 10x in the next five years is feels like it's again reckless and but I think that that's going to happen.

insights.claim: Munster, a tech analyst at Piper from 1995 through the dot-com blowup, argues the right question is not what the business has been historically but the forward demand outlook and who can actually meet it.
insights.speaker: Gene Munster
insights.so_what: A framework for ignoring rearview cyclicality fears; useful lens for evaluating whether AI infra demand is structural.
insights.quote: The the question isn't what has the business been in the past. The question for me is what's the demand outlook going in the future and who is there to to to meet that demand

insights.claim: The real moat in AI infrastructure is execution complexity: chips, memory, data centers and energy are extremely hard to build, which is why startups cannot do it and only a few incumbents will benefit.
insights.speaker: Gene Munster
insights.so_what: Supports concentrating AI infra exposure in a handful of capable incumbents rather than speculative entrants.
insights.quote: This is not something that uh startups can do.

insights.claim: Munster claims we are effectively at AGI now, with models already well beyond average human intelligence, and that broad business recognition of AGI will trigger accelerating adoption rates.
insights.speaker: Gene Munster
insights.so_what: If correct, an adoption inflection is near; the catalyst to watch is hallucination rates falling, not a formal AGI declaration.
insights.quote: I think we're actually at AGI now.

insights.claim: Munster calls the hallucination argument against AI a 'paper tiger,' arguing it will be solved within a year or two and that models already make fewer mistakes than humans in absolute terms.
insights.speaker: Gene Munster
insights.so_what: Declining hallucinations is framed as the gating catalyst for enterprise adoption and the AGI 'light' turning on.
insights.quote: I think that the hallucination argument against AI is um is just basically like a paper tiger.

insights.claim: AI now writing its own code is a watershed: by December Anthropic's Claude Code was writing the code and Anthropic told developers to just manage the machine, with OpenAI following the same path.
insights.speaker: Gene Munster
insights.so_what: Self-coding models are concrete evidence of capability acceleration that underpins the AGI and adoption thesis.
insights.quote: that moved to a point in December when thropic was writing the code was writing the code like that is a huge deal.

insights.claim: Dan Nathan argues the bull case may run through cheap open-source models (e.g., DeepSeek R4) that crush token costs and shift the money to the agentic layer rather than commoditized models, data centers, or infrastructure.
insights.speaker: Dan Nathan
insights.so_what: If value accrues to the agentic layer and tokens commoditize, infra/model owners may underperform application-layer plays.
insights.quote: you can be super bullish about the technology, but you can get there through open-source cheap models that are going to drive down the cost of tokens and then that agentic layer is all going to happen and that's where the money is going to be made.

insights.claim: If Microsoft standardized Copilot on a DeepSeek model it would be a major negative signal that the West accepts Chinese models, which Munster views as unlikely given Microsoft's adversarial China history.
insights.speaker: Gene Munster
insights.so_what: A China-model adoption by a Tier 1 US firm would be a regime-shift signal for US model pricing power; watch for it.
insights.quote: if Microsoft goes... if they standardize on some sort of deepseek version I that would be a major negative

insights.claim: Munster frames the world as bifurcated into China-and-affiliates versus the Western bloc, arguing US tech has thrived without China and the Western AI opportunity alone is large enough.
insights.speaker: Gene Munster
insights.so_what: Implies US AI leaders' upside is largely independent of China access, reducing China-exposure as a thesis risk.
insights.quote: I still operate under the premise that the world is split in two. It's China and its affiliates and then kind of the whole western world.

themes.theme: Google as best AI capital allocator

insights.claim: Munster names Google the best AI capital allocator, having reversed his April 2025 'beginning of the end of Google' call as both Google Cloud growth accelerated and search growth re-accelerated to ~18-19% from 9-11%.
insights.speaker: Gene Munster
insights.so_what: Google is the proof case that AI can lift both infrastructure and a core application business; a held position, though not where the biggest upside lies.
insights.quote: search has gone from a 9 10 11% growing business. I think it was 18 or 19 last quarter. Like that's amazing.

insights.claim: Google Cloud growth accelerated dramatically, with Munster citing a jump from ~25% growth toward 50-60%+ growth, evidence of real-scale AI monetization.
insights.speaker: Gene Munster
insights.so_what: Cloud acceleration validates that hyperscaler AI capex is converting to revenue at incumbents with scale.
insights.quote: going from 25% growth to what is it 50 60% growth

insights.claim: The biggest stock upside comes from a narrative change, which Google captured over the past 18 months; Deep Water still owns Google but no longer sees it as where the big upside lies.
insights.speaker: Gene Munster
insights.so_what: Hunt for the next narrative-change candidate rather than already-re-rated winners to capture outsized returns.
insights.quote: to get big upside, you got to find the companies that get a narrative change. That's what Google had over the past year and a half.

insights.claim: Meta has not figured out how to deploy its own frontier model and lacks a cloud business, leaving its AI monetization unproven relative to Google.
insights.speaker: Gene Munster
insights.so_what: Frames Meta's AI payoff as still a show-me story, a potential catch-up opportunity or a relative laggard.
insights.quote: they haven't figured out how to use the kind of their own frontier model yet... they don't have a cloud business yet

insights.claim: Guy Adami argues Walmart's premium valuation is justified by its ability to implement and leverage AI for significant margin improvement, citing it as a non-tech AI winner.
insights.speaker: Guy Adami
insights.so_what: Points to AI adopters outside tech as an underappreciated source of margin-driven re-rating.
insights.quote: the reason why Walmart's getting the valuation that they get... is their ability to implement and leverage AI, which is why I think their margin improvement has been significant

insights.claim: Apple's product price increases announced the day after Micron reported average roughly +15% (with iPhone, ~50% of the business, up 20-25%), far above the ~5% Munster expected two weeks earlier, driven by surging memory costs.
insights.speaker: Gene Munster
insights.so_what: Quantifies how Micron's pricing power flows downstream to Apple's BOM and tests Apple's ability to pass it through.
insights.quote: I think the average weighted increase of their products is going to be something like plus 15%. If you'd asked me two weeks ago, I would have said they're going to raise it by five.

insights.claim: Munster argues the market is largely getting the Apple price-increase news wrong and that it is directionally positive, potentially lifting fiscal 2027 revenue growth from the Street's ~8% to ~15%, a ~$34B incremental lift on ~$530B base.
insights.speaker: Gene Munster
insights.so_what: A contrarian read on a stock down ~5% intraday; Apple is his preferred name for a narrative-change catalyst.
insights.quote: I think the news today around the price increase is is directionally a positive I think the market's largely getting that wrong

insights.claim: Apple's gross margin has risen every year from 39% in 2016 to 46.9% in the March quarter, and as the world's biggest buyer paying below list BOM prices, Munster expects most of the price increase to defend rather than expand margins.
insights.speaker: Gene Munster
insights.so_what: Apple's scale leverage lets it raise prices less than memory costs rise; margin defense, not expansion, is the realistic base case.
insights.quote: 2016 their gross margin was 39%. It's gone up every year. It's now 46.9% in the most recently reported quarter

insights.claim: Munster's price-elasticity model assumes a 15% average price increase causes only a ~5% negative hit to units, supporting his ~$34B revenue uplift estimate.
insights.speaker: Gene Munster
insights.so_what: The elasticity assumption is the crux; if real demand is more elastic than modeled, the bull case breaks.
insights.quote: a 15% increase in price average has a 5% negative negative impact on units

insights.claim: The key downside risk to Apple is a repeat of the 2018-2020 episode where price increases lengthen the upgrade cycle (e.g., Mac replacement from 4.5 to 4.7 years), which Munster's math shows can be a meaningful ~5% headwind.
insights.speaker: Gene Munster
insights.so_what: Upgrade-cycle elongation, not customer defection, is the real risk to monitor when Apple raises prices.
insights.quote: What they're going to say is I got to hold on to my computer for six more months.

insights.claim: Munster reframes a $200 Mac price hike as only ~$3.70 added to the daily cost of ownership, arguing high product utility means most consumers stay in step despite higher prices.
insights.speaker: Gene Munster
insights.so_what: A utility-per-day lens supports the inelastic-demand thesis underpinning Apple's pricing power.
insights.quote: they raise the price... of a Mac by 200 bucks. That adds about $3.70 to the daily price.

insights.claim: A growing refurbished-phone business sends traded-in iPhones primarily to Eastern Europe and Africa via third parties, with Best Buy newly entering the refurb marketplace through its Geek Squad channel.
insights.speaker: Gene Munster
insights.so_what: Secondary-market dynamics support Apple's trade-in economics and reveal a growing refurb revenue line at Best Buy.
insights.quote: they typically go to um Europe, West, Eastern Europe and and Africa. That's where most of them go.

insights.claim: Munster expects SpaceX's stock to be judged over its first year, not its first two weeks, noting index buying hasn't delivered the hoped-for support and staged secondary sales could pressure the stock near term.
insights.speaker: Gene Munster
insights.so_what: Expect near-term overhang from secondary supply; separate the trading setup from the long-term sovereign-AI thesis.
insights.quote: this index buying is not having the impact that I think a lot of people were hoping for.

insights.claim: SpaceX priced at $135, traded up briefly then back to ~$152, while early holders with cost bases in single dollars to ~$20 still sit on large gains even if the stock breaks deal price.
insights.speaker: Gene Munster
insights.so_what: Low insider cost basis means continued selling pressure is rational and could break deal price without changing the fundamental story.
insights.quote: keep in mind a lot of people's cost basis in this is single dollars teens 20

insights.claim: Munster frames SpaceX as a sovereign-AI play whose unique resources and assets align with where the world is going, urging investors to separate the company's value from views on Elon Musk.
insights.speaker: Gene Munster
insights.so_what: Positions SpaceX as a structural long-term holding tied to the sovereign-AI theme rather than a near-term trade.
insights.quote: it was related to kind of the sovereign AI play... the kind of the long-term goal of what they're trying to build is is just so unique

insights.claim: Munster will bet that Tesla becomes part of SpaceX within the next three years, rolling the publicly traded company under the SpaceX umbrella.
insights.speaker: Gene Munster
insights.so_what: A specific, datable structural bet; a Tesla-SpaceX combination would reshape how to value both entities.
insights.quote: I'll take that that Tesla's part of SpaceX in the next three years.

insights.claim: Munster argues Tesla FSD has improved remarkably with little to no interventions over the past six months and that Tesla data shows FSD is seven times safer than human drivers.
insights.speaker: Gene Munster
insights.so_what: If FSD safety claims hold, autonomy adoption is a multi-year tailwind for Tesla's robotaxi fleet thesis.
insights.quote: they talk about FSD being seven times safer than than what um what what humans are.

insights.claim: Autonomy is extremely early: FSD covers ~0.5% of the ~3.5 trillion annual US passenger miles, and autonomous rides (Uber, Lyft, Waymo, robotaxi) will be only ~2% of total ride shares this year.
insights.speaker: Gene Munster
insights.so_what: Tiny current penetration frames autonomy as a long runway; even conservative adoption implies large multi-year growth.
insights.quote: There's three and a half trillion miles driven a year by passenger cars in the US. So it's about a half a percent.

insights.claim: Munster argues robotaxi timing will naturally slip but the question is whether the hope stays strong, and that investors will be willing to wait it out because society will demand safer autonomous driving.
insights.speaker: Gene Munster
insights.so_what: Reframes autonomy as a patience trade where narrative durability matters more than hitting specific launch dates.
insights.quote: I think the timing gets pushed out naturally... I think investors will be willing to wait it out.

themes.theme: What's next: personalized AI & Oracle's bet

insights.claim: Munster says the next big underappreciated theme is personalized AI with personal context and the ability to spend money on a user's behalf, expecting it to accelerate the consumer-tech hardware cycle around 2028.
insights.speaker: Gene Munster
insights.so_what: Flags personalized AI as the next narrative-change catalyst and a 2028 consumer-tech upgrade driver to position ahead of.
insights.quote: the what's next is personalized AI... the ability to spend money on your behalf.

insights.claim: Munster distinguishes an 'adult table' of incumbents (Google, hyperscalers) with proven scale from a 'kids table' (Oracle, Intel) whose ability to execute AI infrastructure is still unproven.
insights.speaker: Gene Munster
insights.so_what: A sorting framework for AI infra exposure; favors proven-scale incumbents over aspirants until execution is demonstrated.
insights.quote: we've recently kind of referred of the adult table and then the card table that the kids have been at. Oracle has been at the the kids table. Uh so has Intel

insights.claim: Munster expects Oracle's business to clearly benefit from AI infrastructure demand even though he is non-committal on whether the stock is a good buy here.
insights.speaker: Gene Munster
insights.so_what: Separates Oracle's business upside from its stock attractiveness; the infrastructure shortfall ensures demand spillover.
insights.quote: I don't know if the stock is necessarily a good buy here, but their business will clearly benefit

insights.claim: Munster reduces the entire AI debate to one belief test: whether AI's substance will exceed the hype, with the foundational layer being long-term belief and the volatile top layer being short-term spook-driven swings.
insights.speaker: Gene Munster
insights.so_what: A clean mental model for parsing AI-trade volatility: distinguish durable belief from headline-driven drawdowns.
insights.quote: do you believe that the the substance of AI will exceed the hype
